title: Is Sarcasm Detection A Step-by-Step Reasoning Process in Large Language Models?
research_questions: Is human sarcasm detection a step-by-step reasoning process?

datasets_used.name: MUStARD
datasets_used.size: 690 samples total of 3,000 utterances; Avg. Length 14; #Train 552; #Dev -; #Test 138

methods_proposed.name: SarcasmCue
methods_proposed.summary: A prompting framework for sarcasm detection that compares sequential and non-sequential ways of using cues. It contains chain of contradiction, graph of cues, bagging of cues, and tensor of cues.

methods_proposed.name: Chain of Contradiction (CoC)
methods_proposed.summary: A linear CoT-style method that asks the LLM to identify surface sentiment, deduce true intention, and then judge whether the two are inconsistent. It operationalizes sarcasm as contradiction between apparent sentiment and intended meaning.

methods_proposed.name: Graph of Cues (GoC)
methods_proposed.summary: A graph-based prompting method in which ten linguistic, contextual, and emotional cues are vertices and LLM voting greedily selects the most valuable next cue until a confidence threshold or cue limit is reached. It remains a step-by-step method but is not restricted to a single fixed chain.

methods_proposed.name: Bagging of Cues (BoC)
methods_proposed.summary: A non-sequential ensemble prompting method that randomly samples multiple subsets from a pool of ten cues, obtains independent LLM predictions from each subset, and aggregates them by majority vote.

methods_proposed.name: Tensor of Cues (ToC)
methods_proposed.summary: A non-sequential method that extracts linguistic, contextual, and emotional cues, takes final hidden-layer embeddings for each view, fuses them through a token-wise tensor product, and prepends the fused cue sequence to the sarcasm prompt. The LLM is frozen and the fully connected projection layers are trained with cross-entropy loss.

humor_theories.detail: The paper treats sarcasm's quintessential property as the contradiction or incongruity between surface sentiment and true intention; CoC explicitly extracts both and predicts sarcasm when they do not align.

evaluation_methods.detail: Accuracy and Macro-F1 are reported on IAC-V1, IAC-V2, SemEval 2018 Task 3, and MUStARD; the table also reports Avg. of F1 and marks significance improvement over the best baseline via unpaired t-test (p < 0.05).
Accuracy and Macro-F1 are reported for the humor-detection extension on CMMA and UR-FUNNY-V2.

headline_results.finding: On GPT-4o, CoC achieved the best average Macro-F1 among the reported methods and improved over the best prompting baseline ToT.
On Claude 3.5 Sonnet, CoC achieved the best average Macro-F1 and improved over the best baseline IO.
On Llama 3-8B, ToC substantially outperformed the best baseline ToT.
On Qwen 2-7B, ToC substantially outperformed the best baseline IO.
In the humor-detection extension with GPT-4o as backbone, BoC achieved the best average F1 across CMMA and UR-FUNNY-V2.

key_findings: SarcasmCue consistently outperforms the paper's SoTA prompting baselines, with reported F1 improvements of 4.2%, 2.0%, 29.7%, and 58.2%.
For stronger proprietary LLMs, especially GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, sequential cue methods such as CoC and GoC are often strongest, with Claude 3.5 Sonnet + CoC reaching 74.74 Avg. of F1.
For smaller open-source LLMs, the non-sequential ToC method is strongest, reaching 65.24 Avg. of F1 on Llama 3-8B and 68.39 Avg. of F1 on Qwen 2-7B.

The paper concludes that the comparison between sequential and non-sequential strategies does not provide clear empirical evidence that sarcasm detection must be step-by-step, but the Llama 3-8B and Qwen 2-7B results support a non-sequential nature.
The ablation study reports that removing any single cue type hurts performance, with linguistic cues having the most significant impact across most settings.
The error analysis finds that CoC, GoC, and BoC have higher false positive rates, while ToC has the lowest overall error rate and more balanced false positive and false negative rates.
limitations: The paper states that SarcasmCue incorporates only three types of cues, while other potentially useful cues are not integrated.
ToC requires access to the LLM structure and supervised training of additional neural layers, so the paper implements it only for open-source LLMs rather than GPT-4o or Claude 3.5 Sonnet.
For MUStARD, the paper uses only textual information despite the dataset being multimodal.
The case study notes that highly ambiguous situations may require a blend of methods or additional contextual information.

human_samples.population: Producers/speakers of texts in pre-existing sarcasm benchmarks: IAC-V1/IAC-V2 online debates and discussions, SemEval 2018 Task 3 tweets, and MUStARD TV-show conversations.
human_samples.recruitment: No new human recruitment by this paper; the authors selected existing benchmark datasets.
languages: English explicitly for SemEval 2018 Task 3; language for the other sarcasm benchmarks is not explicitly reported in this paper
human_samples.observations: Reported dataset item splits: IAC-V1 #Train 1,595, #Dev 80, #Test 320; IAC-V2 #Train 5,216, #Dev 262, #Test 1,042; SemEval 2018 #Train 3,634, #Dev 200, #Test 784; MUStARD #Train 552, #Test 138, and 690 samples total of 3,000 utterances. These are item/utterance counts, not participant counts.
human_samples.detail: The paper reports no contributor/speaker counts, demographics, or recruitment details for these datasets. For MUStARD, the paper uses only textual information despite the dataset being multimodal.

human_samples.population: Manual annotators of the SemEval 2018 Task 3 irony/sarcasm tweet corpus.
human_samples.observations: The paper states that SemEval 2018 Task 3 was collected using irony-related hashtags and subsequently manually annotated; no annotation count is reported beyond dataset item splits.
human_samples.detail: Annotator count, expertise, agreement, and blinding are not reported in this paper.

human_samples.population: Producers/speakers in pre-existing humor-detection benchmarks CMMA and UR-FUNNY-V2.
human_samples.recruitment: No new human recruitment by this paper; the humor extension uses existing benchmarks.
languages: Chinese for CMMA, from the referenced dataset title; language for UR-FUNNY-V2 is not explicitly reported in this paper
human_samples.observations: Table 4 reports accuracy and Macro-F1 on CMMA and UR-FUNNY-V2 but does not report item counts or participant counts for these two datasets.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not describe contributor counts, demographics, annotator counts, or annotation protocols for the humor datasets.

judges.name: Pre-existing benchmark gold labels
judges.detail: Gold labels from IAC-V1, IAC-V2, SemEval 2018 Task 3, MUStARD, CMMA, and UR-FUNNY-V2 determine correctness. The paper explicitly describes SemEval as manually annotated; label-generation protocols for the other benchmarks are not described in this paper.

judges.name: Accuracy, Macro-F1, Avg. of F1, unpaired t-test
judges.detail: The reported results are computed by accuracy and Macro-F1 against benchmark labels; tables also average F1 across datasets. Table 2 marks significance improvements over the best baseline via an unpaired t-test with p < 0.05.

judges.name: Backbone LLM used as GoC cue evaluator/voter
judges.detail: In Graph of Cues, an LLM acts as the cue evaluator, voting on the most valuable next cue and assessing whether cumulative cues provide sufficient confidence. This affects the method’s internal path selection, not the external benchmark metric.

generation_selection.scope: IO, CoT, and Chain of Contradiction prompting for sarcasm detection
generation_selection.n: one final label per input; CoC uses three sequential steps/generations
generation_selection.detail: No best-of-N output selection is reported. CoC asks for surface sentiment, true intention, then a final consistency/label decision.

generation_selection.n: up to 10 cue-selection iterations are described; exact number of votes/generations per iteration is not reported
generation_selection.detail: The LLM cue evaluator greedily selects cues until confidence is at least 0.95 or the cue limit is reached, then produces a final judgment. This is not reported as best-of-N final-output selection.

generation_selection.n: T cue subsets and q cues per subset are defined but their numerical values are not reported
generation_selection.detail: BoC generates T independent predictions from randomly sampled cue subsets and aggregates them by majority vote. This is an ensemble/voting procedure, not best-of-N selection of the best candidate.

generation_selection.n: one yes/no label decoded from the first generated-token logit
generation_selection.detail: ToC extracts linguistic, contextual, and emotional cues, fuses hidden-layer cue embeddings through a tensor product, prepends the fused cue sequence to the prompt, and decodes a single-token label.
The paper includes ToT as a baseline and says LangChain is used, but it does not report ToT branching factor, depth, evaluator, stopping rule, or candidate-selection procedure.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Implemented via the official OpenAI Python API library. No API model snapshot, version string, or inference date is reported. ToC is not implemented for GPT-4o.

model_provenance.name: Claude 3.5 Sonnet
model_provenance.detail: Implemented via the official Anthropic Python API library. No dated Claude 3.5 Sonnet snapshot/version or inference date is reported. ToC is not implemented for Claude 3.5 Sonnet.

model_provenance.name: LLaMA3-8B-Instruct
model_provenance.detail: Implemented with Hugging Face Transformers; Table 2 labels it Llama 3-8B. No Hugging Face model ID revision, checkpoint hash, quantization, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen 2-7B
model_provenance.detail: Implemented with Hugging Face Transformers. No exact checkpoint/revision, instruction-tuning status, quantization, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen 2-1.5B
model_provenance.detail: Run in the model-scale study for CoC, GoC, BoC, and ToC. Exact checkpoint/revision and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Qwen 2-72B
model_provenance.detail: Run in the model-scale study for CoC, GoC, and BoC. Exact checkpoint/revision, quantization, and inference date are not reported.

model_provenance.name: LLaMA 3-70B
model_provenance.detail: Run in the model-scale study for CoC, GoC, and BoC. Exact checkpoint/revision, instruction-tuning status, quantization, and inference date are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: 3 sequential LLM steps are specified per input: surface sentiment, true intention, and consistency/final label.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: The paper says all models are run on four A100 GPUs, while GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet are accessed via official APIs; cost is not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Used in zero-shot Table 2 and few-shot Table 6. Exact decoding settings, prompt formatting beyond examples, context limits, retries, and output parsing are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Iterative cue-selection loop until confidence >= 0.95 or j <= 10, plus final judgment; exact number of LLM calls/votes per item is not reported.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: up to 10 selected cues; no best-of-N final-output candidates reported
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Four A100 GPUs are reported generally; API calls for GPT-4o/Claude are used, but cost is not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The cue evaluator is an LLM. The implementation uses LangChain, but voting count, stopping implementation, confidence calibration, and generation parameters are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: T independent LLM predictions per input are defined, but T is not numerically reported.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: T predictions aggregated by majority vote; q cues per subset is defined but not reported
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Four A100 GPUs are reported generally; API costs are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Cue subsets are randomly sampled without replacement. Random seeds and the values of T and q are not provided.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: The paper says the LLM extracts linguistic, contextual, and emotional cues respectively and then performs sarcasm detection with tensor-fused cue embeddings; the exact number of generation/forward passes per item is not fully specified.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: one yes/no label from the first generated-token logit
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Training and local inference are reported on four A100 GPUs; cost and runtime are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: ToC freezes the LLM and trains fully connected projection layers using cross-entropy loss. Learning rate, epochs, batch size, optimizer, checkpoint selection, and tensor dimensions beyond the formula are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: method-dependent; see CoC/GoC/BoC/ToC rows. IO, CoT, and ToT call budgets are not specified.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: not reported for ToT; otherwise one final label or BoC majority vote as above
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Mean performance over 5 runs is reported; four A100 GPUs are reported generally.
inference_budgets.detail: Table 2 states that LLM strategies are zero-shot. This is in tension with ToC requiring supervised training of added layers; the paper does not fully reconcile this.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: same method-dependent budgets as CoC, GoC, and BoC
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Mean performance over 5 runs is reported; compute cost not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper evaluates k={0,1,5,10} shots by randomly sampling k examples from the training set. Sampling seeds and exact demonstrations are not reported.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: method-dependent for CoC, GoC, and BoC; exact budgets not separately reported
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: not reported beyond the method definitions
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: GPT-4o is the backbone for SarcasmCue in this extension; API cost is not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Table 4 evaluates CMMA and UR-FUNNY-V2 against MFN and SVM+BERT baselines, but it does not report whether those supervised baselines were rerun or copied from prior work.

human_baselines.scope: Sarcasm detection on IAC-V1, IAC-V2, SemEval 2018, and MUStARD
human_baselines.description: No human performance baseline is reported; human-provided benchmark labels are used only as gold labels for evaluation.
human_baselines.detail: All headline comparisons are between LLM prompting methods and model baselines using automatic metrics.

human_baselines.scope: Humor detection on CMMA and UR-FUNNY-V2
human_baselines.description: No human performance baseline is reported.
human_baselines.detail: The extension compares SarcasmCue with MFN and SVM+BERT model baselines.

contamination_risk.basis: The main sarcasm benchmarks are public legacy datasets from 2013, 2016, 2018, and 2019, and UR-FUNNY is also pre-existing; they are evaluated with later proprietary and open LLMs without reported data-contamination checks, private held-out items, or model snapshot details. CMMA timing relative to all model pretraining is less clear from this paper, but the headline sarcasm results rely on public benchmarks with no decontamination discussion.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: GoC performance and any conclusions involving GoC depend partly on an LLM cue evaluator/voting mechanism; ToT baseline selection details are unclear. Headline task performance, however, is scored by automatic metrics against benchmark gold labels rather than by an external LLM judge.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Only pre-existing benchmark labels are used; no new human validation is reported.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLMs are primarily systems under test. The only explicit LLM-as-evaluator role is internal to GoC cue selection/confidence assessment, not the final evaluation metric.

reporting_gaps: No API snapshots, exact model version strings, Hugging Face checkpoint revisions, quantization settings, or inference dates are reported for any LLM.
Temperature, top-p/top-k, max tokens, stop sequences, context-window handling, retry policy, and output-parsing rules are not reported.
BoC defines T cue subsets and q cues per subset but does not report the numerical values used.
GoC does not report the number of LLM votes/candidates per cue-selection step, confidence-calibration details, or exact stopping implementation beyond the 0.95 threshold and cue limit.
ToT baseline configuration is not reported, including branching factor, depth, evaluator/selector, number of candidates, and stopping rule.
ToC training details are incomplete: optimizer, learning rate, epochs, batch size, validation/checkpoint selection, tensor projection dimensions, and train/dev usage are not reported.
The paper reports mean performance over 5 runs but generally omits random seeds and per-run results; standard deviations appear in figures but not in the main tables.
The exact full prompts are only partially shown through examples and algorithms; complete prompt templates for all datasets/methods are not included in the paper text.
Benchmark annotator counts, annotator demographics, annotation agreement, and blinding are not reported in this paper; only SemEval is explicitly described as manually annotated.
No data-contamination or benchmark memorization analysis is reported.
For the humor extension, the paper does not report CMMA/UR-FUNNY-V2 item counts and does not clarify whether MFN and SVM+BERT baselines were rerun or taken from prior work.

Compute reporting is ambiguous for proprietary API models: the paper says GPT-4o and Claude use official APIs but also says all models are run on four A100 GPUs; API cost and local runtime are not reported.
